perturbation.original_text: Pey Dirt agrees that any material, advertising or otherwise, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by Company to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved in writing within fourteen (14) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Pey Dirt agrees that any material, advertising or otherwise, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by Company to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved in writing within three (3) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval period to 3 days might be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially conflicting with fair business practice laws or general contract law principles that require reasonable opportunity for review and consideration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the approval period to 3 days can be seen as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it does not allow Pey Dirt a reasonable amount of time to review and disapprove the marketing content if necessary.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the approval period from 14 to 3 days, which the LLM explanation and the perturbation explanation both identify as a potential breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, because it might not allow a reasonable amount of time for review. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By reducing the time to only 3 days, the changed text creates a potential conflict with the standard of 'fair dealing', which is an element of good faith. This makes the contract potentially unenforceable because it may be seen as not allowing Pey Dirt a reasonable opportunity for review.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, general liability insurance and product liability insurance with limits no less than $3,000,000 and within thirty (30) days from the date hereof, Company will submit to Pey Dirt a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Pey Dirt, Pey Dirt's agent and Manning as additional insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Pey Dirt at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, general liability insurance and product liability insurance with limits no less than $3,000,000 and within sixty (60) days from the date hereof, Company will submit to Pey Dirt a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Pey Dirt, Pey Dirt's agent and Manning as additional insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Pey Dirt at least ten (10) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deadline for insurance submission to sixty days and reducing the advance notice for termination to ten days increases Pey Dirt's exposure to potential liability during the initial period and upon policy changes. The change involving termination notice could violate standards related to adequate notice periods for insurance policy changes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Code § 56-7-108. Notice of cancellation or nonrenewal
perturbation.law_explanation: While this statute primarily concerns auto insurance, it exemplifies the principle that adequate notice must be provided before an insurance policy is canceled or significantly altered. Reducing the notice period to ten days might not provide Pey Dirt with sufficient time to secure alternative coverage, potentially leading to a lapse in required insurance protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Tennessee Code § 56-7-108 (2021) - Mortality Tables :: 2021 Tennessee Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Tennessee Code 2023 Tennessee Code 2021 Tennessee Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2021 Tennessee Code Title 56 - Insurance Chapter 7 - Policies and Policyholders Part 1 - General Provisions § 56-7-108. Mortality Tables Universal Citation: TN Code § 56-7-108 (2021) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Notwithstanding any other law, the mortality tables adopted by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) for life insurance contracts may be used by insurers to determine adjusted premiums, present values and reserve values, within any limitations of application as specified by NAIC. Mortality tables adopted by NAIC may be used by insurers pursuant to this section beginning on or after January 1 of the year following adoption by NAIC, unless the commissioner rejects the table by issuing a bulletin posted on the department's web site and mailed to all domestic companies holding a certificate of authority to write life insurance in the state. A mortality table rejected by the commissioner may be subsequently adopted or modified by the commissioner pursuant to a rulemaking in accordance with the Uniform Administrative Procedures Act, compiled in title 4, chapter 5. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the advance notice for termination of insurance from twenty (20) to ten (10) days. The Law Explanation indicates this might not provide sufficient time to secure alternative coverage, potentially leading to a lapse in required insurance protection. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about mortality tables and doesn't directly address insurance cancellation notice periods, the Law Explanation uses an example of auto insurance to highlight the general legal principle that adequate notice must be provided before an insurance policy is significantly altered. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by suggesting the change could violate standards related to adequate notice periods for insurance policy changes. Because of this violation, there is a legal contradiction.
